personal.name: Ángel Roldán Rabanal
personal.title: Multiplatform Application Developer
personal.tagline: Creative, curious developer — I like things that work beautifully in production. Mostly PHP, SQL and JavaScript on web and mobile; this site is my Next.js lab. Based in Seville; Erasmus+ in Prague was my 2026 international chapter. Always learning.
personal.closing: Driving licence · fine with travel when it makes sense — write if you want to compare notes or talk roles.

about: Passionate, creative multiplatform application developer — I completed the Higher Vocational Degree in Multiplatform Application Development at SAFA (May 2026). I ship web and mobile work with PHP, JavaScript, SQL and more: user-centred delivery is still the thread through my GitHub and paid roles. I love building things that work beautifully, learning fast, and contributing to real projects with genuine curiosity. I like systems that stay understandable — backends you can reason about, front-ends that do not fight the user, integrations you can reopen months later. Erasmus+ in Prague (Buinsoft, through May 2026) layered AI-assisted workflows and an international cadence on top of the same obsession: how software actually behaves in production.

projects.title: This portfolio (open source)
projects.description: The site you are on: Next.js 16, React Three Fiber, GSAP, Lenis, EN/ES i18n. Source on GitHub; deployed on Vercel.
content: This portfolio doubles as a CV and a lab: I use it to try GSAP motion, Lenis-smooth scrolling, and a lightweight WebGL scene with React Three Fiber, while keeping the story easy to scan and maintain.

The stack is Next.js 16 with the App Router: a single landing with anchored sections plus dedicated project pages. Copy lives in typed JSON (English and Spanish) so text iterations do not fight component code.
The repo is public on purpose — it shows how I structure styles, sections, and data, and how I leave small breadcrumbs so the project still makes sense months later.

highlights.title: Goal
highlights.body: Show real shipping experience and recent experiments with a consistent look, two languages, and frictionless Vercel deploys.

highlights.title: Technical tension
highlights.body: Balance motion and 3D with sensible first-load behaviour, strict typing, and small, focused components.

highlights.title: Status
highlights.body: Active: new sections, copy passes, and small refactors when time allows.

projects.title: TECHNOAPP
projects.description: Social guide for Prague techno events: events, venues, agenda, profiles, friends, chats, and an admin panel. Next.js 16, Firebase, Resident Advisor scraping, AI classification, and an Android build via Capacitor.
content: TECHNOAPP is a social guide for Prague techno events — public routes for events, calendar, profile, social, and chats, with guarded admin tooling behind the scenes.
The App Router exposes APIs for events, venues, agenda, attendees, friendships, chats, and scheduled scraping, with demo/mock mode for safer iteration.
Capacitor scripts support an Android pipeline on top of the web build when a native shell is needed for demos.

highlights.title: Product scope
highlights.body: Events, venues, agenda, profiles, friends, chats, and admin flows in one codebase.

highlights.title: Stack
highlights.body: Next.js 16, Firebase, Resident Advisor scraping, AI classification, and Capacitor for Android output.

highlights.title: Documentation
highlights.body: README still on the Next.js starter — next pass should document Firebase, env vars, admin/scraper flow, and Android commands.

projects.title: A Saturday Night of Angel
projects.description: Unity 2D platformer in C# with menu and level scenes, movement, double jump, shooting, enemy patrols, health, pickups, and game-over state.
content: A small end-of-cycle Unity project in C# — scenes, character or interaction scripts, and the usual compile–play–debug loop of learning the editor properly.
C# scripts cover movement, shooting, enemies, health, pickups, and moving platforms across MainMenu, Nivel1, and Nivel2.
The repository is public so reviews can focus on folder structure, naming, and gameplay code clarity, not only the exported binary.

highlights.title: Context
highlights.body: Academic game-engine exercise useful for showing logic, scenes, and version control discipline.

highlights.title: Code
highlights.body: Modular C# scripts where possible, with comments where they explain design choices.

highlights.title: Documentation
highlights.body: No published README yet — next pass should cover controls, scenes, and build/export notes.

experience.description: Erasmus+ internship in Prague (completed May 2026): internal and client-facing tools with heavy AI-assisted delivery — automation, leaner iterations, hybrid cadence with the team.
Maintenance and small features on PHP/SQL/JS stacks — performance passes, UX fixes, the unglamorous work that keeps a music-industry app humming.
Institutional web work: custom PHP, tuned MySQL, Google and REST integrations — the kind of full-stack glue where the brief changes mid-semester and the data model has to keep up.

heading: A bit
about me
body: I am Ángel — Multiplatform Application Developer out of SAFA Seville, with years of real tickets on institutional and music-industry PHP/SQL/JS stacks, aligned with what I publish on GitHub. I still care about boring reliability: migrations you can replay, queries you can explain, and UIs that do not ambush people who already have a full calendar.

Prague at Buinsoft was my Erasmus+ stretch: internal and client tools with AI-assisted delivery, without pretending fundamentals disappear. This page stays partly CV, partly lab log — scroll for selected work and contact.

items.title: Web apps
items.blurb: PHP and SQL first, JS where the browser needs it — CRUD, auth, reports, the unglamorous middle of a product.

items.title: Mobile & Ionic
items.blurb: Shared Angular + Capacitor builds for things that need to leave the desk — still one codebase to reason about.

items.title: Backends & APIs
items.blurb: Services that talk to MySQL/Oracle without mystery — versioning, logging, and contracts you can grep.

items.title: Data modelling
items.blurb: Schemas and migrations that survive the next feature request — not perfect diagrams, tables that behave.

items.title: Integrations
items.blurb: OAuth, webhooks, cron — glue code that should read like a checklist when you come back in six months.

items.title: Front-end lab
items.blurb: This portfolio: Next.js, motion, a bit of WebGL — experiments that keep my JS/React muscles warm outside PHP day jobs.

heading: Public
repos
works.intro: Public repositories and live demos: this portfolio, TECHNOAPP on Vercel, and A Saturday Night of Angel (Unity, public on GitHub). Click a card for the full write-up; the small icon jumps straight to the external link. Client roles and the Erasmus+ placement at Buinsoft are under Process below as employment experience.

heading: How I
work
process.intro: Not a methodology slide — habits that keep coursework wrap-ups, production tickets, and side experiments from colliding too loudly.

steps.title: Read the system
steps.body: Screenshots, logs, who owns which table. I try to know what already works before renaming half of it.

steps.title: Small slices
steps.body: Commits and PRs you can actually review — backend and UI in parallel so nobody stares at a blank page for weeks.

steps.title: Ship, then watch
steps.body: Staging when there is one, backups when there should be, and a quick sanity pass on real data shapes.

steps.title: Leave breadcrumbs
steps.body: README fragments, ticket notes, a line in the changelog — future me (or the next dev) deserves a map.

stack.heading: Stack in rotation
stack.intro: Languages, frameworks and infra I actually grep docs for — the same mix behind vocational builds, institutional tooling, music-industry tickets, the Erasmus+ sprint in Prague, and this Next.js portfolio.

heading: Say
hello
cta.body: If you want to compare notes on stacks, early-career delivery, or what Erasmus+ in Prague was like in practice — email is best. No pitch deck required; I read messages slowly and answer honestly.
